- It makes the origin of a function, class, etc. very clear at the place where it is actually used.
- It doesn't pollute the current namespace with often generic and ambiguous terms as "data", "filters", "io", ... which are also prone to naming conflicts with similar libraries. E.g. `label`, is it from scikit-image or SciPy? 
- For me it's also a nicer experience when developing or reading code, no need to jump between imports and code as I am adding new functionality from scikit-image.

It is a long story.
Originally every command required explicit cluster name to be specified in the command-line, because it was supposed to work for an arbitrary Patroni clusters registered in DCS.

But, it turned out that people mostly run `patronictl` on the same host where Patroni runs.
Providing the cluster name was very annoying (especially knowing that it is already in the config file), therefore for most commands we made the cluster name optional. If the cluster name isn't supplied, it is picked from the config file.
Known exceptions to this rule are:
1. commands which are followed by a member name, e.g.: `patronictl reinit $CLUSTER_NAME $MEMBER_NAME`.
2. "dangerous" command: `patronictl remove $CLUSTER_NAME`. I call in dangerous because it wipes all keys for a given cluster from DCS.

Since a Citus cluster is effectively just a bunch of different Patroni clusters logically connected using the groupid I had to introduce the `--group` option for all commands. And now we have more or less the same story.
1. For `patronictl list` we probably don't want to use the default group value from the config because typically users want to see all coordinator and worker nodes as a whole.
2. `patronictl remove` command still remains dangerous, and since we require the cluster name to be explicitly specified we have to do the same for the `--group`. Actually, the `remove` command has only an interactive mode and asks to confirm the cluster name. Probably we should do the same for the group.
3. `patronictl switchover/failover` -- the workflow of these commands implies that it works only for a single Patroni cluster, therefore the group must be specified either explicitly from the command line, or it will ask for while working in the interactive mode.

For remaining commands the workflow will stay as it is. Lets say you investigate the problem with one of the workers. In this case most likely you will be on the host where the worker is running and want `patronictl` do some actions (edit-config/show-config/pause/resume) for a given group.

Sorting of documents is defined in appendix E, page 50 and following:
`/** Ordnungszahl für die Sortierung im Auszug **/
AuszugIndex : MANDATORY -1000 .. 1000;`

A lot of the positive range is already blocked by the way the confederation defines it's indexes for the legal bases, e.g.:
> 000 Raumplanung
010 Bundesgesetz über die Raumplanung (SR 700)
100 Strassen
110 Bundesgesetz über die Nationalstrassen (SR 725.11)
120 Nationalstrassenverordnung (SR 725.111)

leaving following ranges for legal bases on state or municipality level:

> 000 Raumplanung
020 bis und mit 099 für kantonale und kommunale gesetzliche Grundlagen der Kategorie Raumplanung
100 Strassen
130 bis und mit 199 für kantonale und kommunale gesetzliche Grundlagen der Kategorie Strassen

In the Extract-model, the Index is also defined. But we can not use only the index to sort the documents, we need a second criteria - either the document type or alphabetical order on the title or something else.

Why? - It may happen that you have more legal bases on cantonal level than the available index range for a theme (but unlikely)
But we need to sort the legal dispositions too and there obviously exist more documents for some themes than the available ranges.
So, I'd suggest we use the document type & index as the sort criteria which allows us to use the complete range from -1000 to 1000 for other documents than legal bases. - I'm still unsure wheter 2000 positions are sufficient for large cantons in the Land Use Plans theme...

This `copy=True` here (and elsewhere in the PR) was to avoid a smaller-scope version of this Arrow serialization bug in `pyarrow <= 6.0.1` that we observed a while back, where the zero-copy slice views produced by `copy=False` would result in the entire `block` buffer to be sent for every slice. To circumvent this, we eagerly copied the block slices.

With this Arrow serialization hook, this copy (i.e. "buffer truncation", where the zero-copy slice view is turned into a truncated copy of the corresponding slice of the buffer) will happen automatically at serialization time, so we don't need to explicitly copy the slices here.

I went ahead and removed this logic, as well. Although it's technically a breaking change in how CodaLab treats certain bundles, I don't think we want to keep it because the current behavior is problematic.

Current behavior:
- If you upload an archive to CodaLab with exactly two files: `README` and `.DS_Store`, CodaLab will just treat the bundle as a single file called `README`.
- If you upload an archive to CodaLab with exactly three files: `README`, `README2`, and `.DS_Store`, CodaLab will treat the bundle as a folder with three files: `README`, `README2`, and `.DS_Store`.

New behavior (with this PR):
- If you upload an archive to CodaLab with exactly two files: `README` and `.DS_Store`, CodaLab will treat the bundle as a folder with two files: `README` and `.DS_Store`.
- If you upload an archive to CodaLab with exactly three files: `README`, `README2`, and `.DS_Store`, CodaLab will treat the bundle as a folder with three files: `README`, `README2`, and `.DS_Store`.

For the two-file scenario, the current behavior is problematic because the user **has already included the .DS_Store file** in the fileobj they are uploading. Presumably, if they really wanted to ignore `.DS_Store`, they would have done so with `cl upload --exclude-patterns`, and the fileobj wouldn't have included `.DS_Store` in the first place. Because fileobj _does_ include both these files, the new behavior in this PR makes the most sense.

Moreover, note the inconsistency in the current behavior -- `.DS_Store` is removed from the final bundle _only in_ the 2-files case, but it is included in the 3-files case. This also shows why the current behavior isn't ideal.

I removed this logic now so I wouldn't have to replicate this kind of problematic / unnecessary logic for Blob Storage (thus simplifying blob storage implementation).

These scenarios are also covered in the `test_fileobj_single_tar_gz_with_dsstore_should_not_simplify_archive` and `test_fileobj_single_tar_gz_with_dsstore_should_not_simplify_archive_2` test cases.

This should always be 0. Going from the discussion given in data-apis/dataframe-api#67, the `offset` value indicates the offset in the actual buffers that are being returned from `get_buffers()`. 

For instance, if all the chunks are backed by a single buffer then `.get_buffers()` return the same for each chunk, when the `offset` indicates the number of bits (`offset * dtype.bit_width`) to start interpreting the data from in this particular chunk. Since in ModinOnPandas implementation chunks are independent buffers there is no such thing as an offset.

Some of the responses on that Stack Overflow thread say that setting `SUFFIXES` can be dangerous so I'd like to avoid it.

I see this in the `find_library()` docs:

> NAMES
Specify one or more possible names for the library.

> When using this to specify names with and without a version suffix, we recommend specifying the unversioned name first so that locally-built packages can be found before those provided by distributions.

> Each library name given to the NAMES option is first considered as a library file name and then considered with platform-specific prefixes (e.g. lib) and suffixes (e.g. .so). Therefore one may specify library file names such as libfoo.a directly. This can be used to locate static libraries on UNIX-like systems.

Taken together, I think we can do `NAMES R R.dll`, which means that libraries will be searched in this order on Windows

1. `R`
2. `R.dll`
3. `libR.so`
4.  `libR.dll.so` (yes it's nonsense but it costs us nothing)

Regarding making the inner value optional: I think I had an interesting thought in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9634#discussion_r416121567

Basically: I think that it's possible that the SourcesRequest could handle _all_ of the casting in one go... so then it would basically be: "get me this target as either an X, Y, or Z"... then the caller would switch on the possibilities.

The advantage to this is that you almost ~never want to treat a field as multiple types in one consumer (ie, it's very unlikely for me to want a dependency as both Files and PythonSources), so the caller will want to do something like: `"for each dependency, get as exactly one of these field types, or None if I should ignore it"`.

I'm not sure if I think it's great that using `--debug` alters Django settings: I have been thinking more about scenarios for using it to debug the production environments, giving instructions to users to run with `--debug` or add `KOLIBRI_DEBUG` in their environment.

Django's DEBUG has stronger semantics as a development environment flag, for instance it switches on template debugging now. In this case, it doesn't enable loading and serving static files from app folders, though.. so that's nice... but perhaps also unexpected!

`settings.DEBUG=True` mixes development environment stuff into the picture, which could be disturbing and perhaps create unwanted troubleshooting issues... you all know how it is, when suddenly some unexpected development thing is enabled and vice versa :)

>The interface to customize a srcset is provided through `create_srcset` so I'm curious what the reasoning would be for a user to want to call this by itself? 

Reasons:
* allow users to figure out what `target_widths` does without having to do everything else (i.e. define a builder, create a url etc.)
  * the function itself doesn't depend on any configuration other than what's passed in
  * moreover `create_srcset` only relies on what's passed in as well, however, one of the things that's passed is `self`

So, `create_srcset` is coupled with the class, but it doesn't need to be. It doesn't mutate its internal state. By this I mean it doesn't mutate any of these
```python
  self._domain = domain
  self._sign_key = sign_key
  self._use_https = use_https
  self._include_library_param = include_library_param
```
The class does call function that are internal to its implementation, i.e.
https://github.com/imgix/imgix-python/blob/2920949dd752f85c8f6b764f4092a0d0e66ba942/imgix/urlbuilder.py#L150

But in this case as well, the functions don't need access to internal state other than to call functions that also do not rely on modifying internal state.  If `target_widths` needed to know about or mutate some state that's internal to `class UrlBuilder`, e.g. `self.domain`, then wrapping it up in the class would seem natural. But that's not the case.

I think this needs to be `PUBLIC`. Because the `mbedtls` target includes `mbedtls-config.h` in its public headers (header files in `${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/repo/include`), and `mbedtls-config.h` includes a core config file in `${PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR}/src/core`. So targets depending on `mbedtls` will not be able to find the core config file if we make `${PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR}/src/core` PRIVATE.

even worse, `${OT_PRIVATE_DEFINES}` below also needs to be PUBLIC. Otherwise, targets other than `openthread-ftd` and `openthread-mtd` will see different mbedtls header files.

To avoid exposing `${PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR}/src/core` and `${OT_PRIVATE_DEFINES}`, one option is to provide mbedtls macros by `target_compile_definitions(...)` rather than `MBEDTLS_CONFIG_FILE`. thoughts?

```
<hastexo
  stack_template_path="hot_lab.yaml"

  stack_protocol="ssh">
  <provider name="provider1" capacity="20" environment="hot_env1.yaml" />
  <provider name="provider1" capacity="30" environment="hot_env2.yaml" />
  <%block name="hastexo_tests" />
</hastexo>
<%block name="hastexo_tests"><%text>
  <test>
    #!/bin/bash
    exit 0
  </test>
  <test>
    #!/bin/bash
    exit 0
  </test>
</%text></%block>

To this:

```
<hastexo xmlns:option="http://code.edx.org/xblock/option"

      environment: hot_env1.yaml
    - name: provider2
      capacity: 30
      environment: hot_env2.yaml

> I was trying to avoid having us to create a bunch of these things whenever we want to inspect the mode in a slightly different way.
>
> I think you convinced me though, by me rereading your comment on GH actually. We have defined a one-to-one mapping from mode to ACL for Certbot's purposes. The inverse is not true and we cannot map an arbitrary ACL to a mode, even if we ignore the group permission problems for a second.
>
> When we encounter something that doesn't fit this model, this deviation cannot be communicated to the caller through the mode so we have either throw away the information or raise an exception. Having multiple special purpose functions that don't try to shove everything into a mode allows more flexibility and lets you more accurately check what you actually care about.

So it's engines responsobility to serialize objects in the most efficient way, when their executing a function.

I found an article explaining shear transformation.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shear_mapping

This says 
"the shear factor m is the cotangent of the shear angle $\phi$ between the former verticals and the x-axis."
Is this helpful?

Note that the skimage's version defines the angle $\phi'$ as the angle between the former verticals and the y-axis. So we should translate them with the equation $\phi' = \pi / 2 - \phi$, especially $m = \cot \phi = \tan \phi'$. I don't see one.

`valid_sets` in `LGBMClassifier.fit()` is passed through to `LGBMModel.fit()` via argument `eval_set`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fe15761b683e796cf68d097b301d171bbffd6aff/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L1106

`LGBMModel.fit()` initializes an internal empty list, `valid_sets`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fe15761b683e796cf68d097b301d171bbffd6aff/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L758

If the passed-in `eval_set` not `None`, you're right that *technically* another code path is considered

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fe15761b683e796cf68d097b301d171bbffd6aff/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L759

However, that won't actually do anything differently in the case `eval_set = []` vs. `eval_set = None`, because all the code inside that path is a `for` loop over `eval_set` (and a for loop over an empty list is basically the same as the code inside the `for loop` being skipped entirely).

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fe15761b683e796cf68d097b301d171bbffd6aff/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L773

And after that, whether `eval_set = []` or `eval_set = None`, the `valid_sets` internal variable will still be `[]`, and that's what gets passed through to `lgb.train()`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/fe15761b683e796cf68d097b301d171bbffd6aff/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L811

So unless you see another path, I think the change proposed in this PR is safe and won't be visible to users at all.

The `ValueError` is turned into DRF's `AuthenticationFailed` in [`oidc.ApiTokenAuthentication`](https://github.com/City-of-Helsinki/django-helusers/pull/33/commits/0ecf259ac5540558428d70a266d880d96533f704#diff-33dfa22394312a69b490ba7760a7c35ee221c0ac890fbd364d9783dd89de31ceR52-R55) and in [`jwt.JWTAuthentication`](https://github.com/City-of-Helsinki/django-helusers/pull/33/commits/0ecf259ac5540558428d70a266d880d96533f704#diff-3063b6feea6b8068dd059378dffe9e355fb759fdbc57780a4fe7f1e3900c9710R34-R37).

But the social auth pipeline case is not yet handled. I'm not sure if raising DRF exceptions from the social auth pipeline has ever been the correct thing to do anyway, because the social auth pipeline shouldn't probably depend on DRF either. Any idea what the pipeline should do? The documentation doesn't say much about it. It [mentions](https://python-social-auth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/developer_intro.html#understanding-the-pipeline) that:

> 2. If the function returns a dictionary or something False-ish [--] call the next step in the pipeline [--]
> 3. If something else is returned (for example, a subclass of HttpResponse), then return that to the browser.

In order to interrupt the pipeline, step 3 should be taken. But what that _something else_ could be in this case? `Django-helusers` has no idea in what kind of project it is used in and what kind of responses should be returned to the browser. The documentation doesn't say anything about how exceptions raised in a pipeline function are handled (if at all). Supposedly pipeline functions shouldn't raise any exceptions?

I suppose this hasn't been thought about before. In the default pipeline that `django-helusers` provides, `ensure_uid_is_uuid` ensures that `uid` is set (and is a UUID). Then `create_or_update_user` puts `uid` into the `sub` field. Then `get_or_create_user` is called and because `sub` field is now always set, it doesn't raise that `ValueError` (or previously the DRF `AuthenticationFailed`). So...there's no problem 😲 ?

The AP/DP register accesses should be done using the `DebugPort` methods rather than going through the probe.  You can directly use the AP register access methods on `DebugPort` with a "full" AP register address, such as 0x0200_00FC to read the IDR. The appropriate DP registers will be updated if necessary to select the AP (at offset 24 in the addr) and bank (offset 4).

It's also not necessarily safe to directly call the probe methods, since that bypasses the register cache in `DebugPort`, and it can thus get out of sync.

Ok, so in this query, according to `Explain` there will be 1 main query + 9 sub-queries (although very fast ones, because they will be filtering by primary key) This can be rewritten to just have one join and calculation inside using `CASE WHEN` ```sql select worker_pools.worker_pool_id, worker_pools.provider_id, worker_pools.previous_provider_ids, worker_pools.description, worker_pools.config, worker_pools.created, worker_pools.last_modified, worker_pools.owner, worker_pools.email_on_error, worker_pools.provider_data, coalesce(sum(CASE WHEN workers.state != 'stopped' THEN workers.capacity ELSE 0 END))::integer as non_stopped_capacity, coalesce(sum(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'running' THEN workers.capacity ELSE 0 END))::integer as running_capacity, coalesce(sum(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'requested' THEN workers.capacity ELSE 0 END))::integer as requested_capacity, coalesce(sum(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'stopping' THEN workers.capacity ELSE 0 END))::integer as stopping_capacity, coalesce(sum(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'stopped' THEN workers.capacity ELSE 0 END))::integer as requested_capacity, coalesce(count(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'requested' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END))::integer as requested_workers, coalesce(count(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'running' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END))::integer as running_workers, coalesce(count(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'stopping' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END))::integer as stopping_workers, coalesce(count(CASE WHEN workers.state = 'stopped' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END))::integer as stopped_workers from worker_pools left join workers ON workers.worker_pool_id = worker_pools.worker_pool_id group by worker_pools.worker_pool_id order by worker_pools.worker_pool_id ``` This results in two sequential scans And it feels like doing second one is ~30-50% faster on community tc database (probably because query needs to scan all rows anyways)

- `w` and `b` are in `ModelProto.graph.input` and `ModelProto.graph.initializer`.

- `w` and `b` can be found as keys in `TrainingInfoProto.update_binding`

It is my passionate and not-entirely-uninformed opinion that a library should not muck around with global state, except where it's either somehow scoped to the lib (say, `logging.getLogger(__name__)`) or **really** necessary.

This situation is clearly not the former, and IMO not really the latter... the failure case is both easily detectable by pytest-twisted, and has a clear, easy (enough) to describe resolution. The burden to the end user is a matter of two lines at module scope in `conftest.py` (I think? We should probably test that...), and the benefit is not needing to hunt through other probably-nonsensical errors caused by the global fiddling.

OTOH, my feeling is that the intersection between the userbases of asyncio, twisted, pytest-twisted, and windows is small as it is, and might just vanish entirely when you intersect in Python 3.8 and *people who depend on `ProactorEventLoop`*. I wish it would be feasible to get some actual data on that 😐. Then again, the number will probably only increase.

Personal ideology and guessing aside, I still prefer the `except NotImplementedError` approach over "look at versions" for a more practical reason: version detection sucks. We don't actually care what Python version we're running under for this check, only if the event loop policy will give Twisted an incompatible event loop... and that can happen on pre-3.8.

Ahh, I thought you use pipenv only for the travis because the previous instructions didn't use pipenv either. The thing though, that it is not easy to work with multiple versions of python with pipenv. There is a problem though, pylint has no version which is supports py2.7 up to py3.8, meaning as long as `Pipfile.lock` (or `requirements.txt` for that matter) is used to define `pylints` version the installation will fail. 
I can, however, advise the user to use `--skip-lock` in either one of the versions or even always. 

Though I don't really see the advantage of using `pipenv` in general for development. In a test environment it is nice because it automatically creates the virtualenv. But I guess most developers use either `pyenv` or just create their venv themselves. And in case one wants a version pinning one could use something like `requirements-py3.txt` and `requirements-py2.txt`. But then again even in the tests I am not so sure about version pinnings because you wouldn't see anything when a required package has changed.

> You can also mark it with double underscore

Indeed, however that functionality isn't here for encapsulation, but rather to prevent name collisions. For example if some class has `__x` vatiable in it, and class which overrides it also defined `__x`, it wouldn't be the same variable.

This is because python automatically coverts these double underscored parameters into `_ClassName__parametername`, so for example `__x` in `Foo` class would be accessible from the instance as: `Foo()._Foo__x`.

Many people confuse this behavior with encapsulation, since you can't directly do `Foo.__x`, but as you can see, the variable is in fact still accessible even in this case, it was just renamed to prevent naming collisions.

Note: I had to add a significant grace period, and below the reason for that.
Year is 365.25 days (which is the reason that there are leap years).
Astral takes that into account, so the same day in a different year is not identical in term of the times.
The main problem is that we calculate sun_hour as "(sunset - sunrise) // 12". It means that 59.9 will become 59, which can cause sun_hour of the same-day-different-years to have 1 minute delta (e.g. 59.9 vs 60.1). Now, we multiple this 1 minute difference in other places. For example, small_mincha=sunrise + 9.5 * sun_hour. It makes the delta quite significant.

Since we no longer do the actual arithmetic for time calculation, we can also consider the skip this test when Astral is used, as it tests that library (and not the logic in this library). Anyway, I kept it for now, and it's up to you to decide.

Yes, in the current design, the caller chooses the name.

> I'm also uncomfortable, but I think that's because I don't have a clear idea of what the object service does in response to these requests. Is it just picking the "best" option from requested protocols? In that case, why would the best be any other than the first choice? Doesn't the client have to do some work to pick the request order? If the object server is just picking a protocol, then it seems we could reverse the logic, and the endpoint could return the menu of protocols, and the client could pick the one it likes.
> 
> But, I expect there is some work done to prepare a download via protocol. Some possible work:
> 
>     * An authorized client could be asking for a public URL of a previously private download. The object server copies the file to a public bucket, or updates the metadata
> 
>     * A client could ask for the gzipped version of a file. The object server uploads a gzipped version to a bucket and returns the URL
> 
>     * A client asks for a URL to a private resource with baked-in credentials. The object server generates the URL with credentials in the query string
> 
>     * A client asks for compressed content, with a list of compression formats. The server picks one that is ready to go, rather than re-compresses for the first requested format.
> 
> 
> If this is the kind of work the object server does, then it makes more sense to me that the server picks. It could still be accomplished with a menu of protocol options, but it goes from two to three requests:
> 
>     1. The client requests the metadata, maybe with a caching header for the last time the client asked. The server responds with the menu of protocol options, with data like "I can gzip this, but I haven't yet"
> 
>     2. The client requests the selected protocol, with any protocol-specific options. The server does some work, and responds with the connection details, such as a URL
> 
>     3. The client requests the download
> 
> 
> But, that's probably a different object service. I'm happy to see where this one goes, and what use cases it supports.

Regarding all of these ideas, I think they would be good enhancements for a future version of the object service.

Regarding compression negotiation and signed URLs, we could see it being added in future, as additional fields in the request body of this API method. Although compression could sometimes be negotiated in the download request directly, this may not always be possible, if the backing provider doesn't support it (e.g. we haven't found a way for S3 artifacts to be served supporting multiple content encodings) - so indeed the Object Service could handle this by creating additional S3 objects on-demand in this API call, if a new content encoding is required. Similarly, we could add an expiration property for signed URLs if the backing provider supports it.

For the first issue, I think it is present in the previous code, but it didn't seem to be causing problems--maybe something to do with the scheduler limiting calls to PopWorker based on resources, but I'm not sure.  Through debug print statements I've seen PopWorker get called 2-3 times before the new worker is registered.  It could potentially be more of an issue in the future when we have worker processes that take several minutes to start--for example, if a runtime env conda environment needs to be installed before the worker can start.

The second issue would also be present in the previous code, but it also didn't seem to be causing problems.  It's probably rare or impossible (Idle workers are killed in FIFO order, so all the other workers would have to be killed first).  Also, even if it does happen, it will just start a new worker process, so it's probably not an issue.

I thought that advice about absolute imports referred to Python 2-style implicit relative imports, which were bad and which were removed in Python 3 (relative imports were introduced in Python 2.5).  PEP 8 appears to permit both absolute and explicit relative imports:

> Absolute imports are recommended (...) explicit relative imports are an acceptable alternative (...) implicit relative imports should never be used and have been removed in Python 3.

An absolute import would be:

```from satpy.readers.netcdf_utils import NetCDF4FileHandler```

Personally I tend to prefer relative imports because it makes it more maintainable if packages are refactored, such as when a collection of modules are moved into a different subpackage, but that consideration appears not to be mentioned by PEP 8.

Yeah, this is complicated... what you say makes logical sense, but, openssl's API is super awkward. There isn't any way to say "please decrypt all the data in your receive buffer". You have to pick a value to pass to `SSLObject.read`. And even more annoying: you don't find out until after you've picked a value whether you have to go back to the underlying transport for more data. So you have to pick the value before you know how much data the underlying transport wants to give you. And once you've picked a value, you have to keep using that value until some data is returned.

So my logic was: well, if we already have a bunch of data in the receive buffer because the underlying transport was generous, then likely we can just decrypt and return that, and the size of the encrypted data is a plausible upper bound on the size of the decrypted data, so `self._incoming.pending` is a good value to pass to `SSLObject.read`.

But, sometimes there won't be a lot of data in the receive buffer – for example, because our heuristic worked well the previous time, and cleared everything out, or almost everything. Like, imagine there's 1 byte left in the receive buffer. The way TLS works, you generally can't decrypt just 1 byte – everything's transmitted in frames, and you need to get the whole frame with its header and MAC and everything before you can decrypt any of it. So if we call `ssl_object.read(1)`, then openssl will end up requesting another large chunk of data from the underlying transport, then our `read(1)` call will decrypt the first byte and return it, leaving the rest of the data sitting in the buffer for next time. And that would be unfortunate.

So my first attempt at a heuristic is: use the receive buffer size, but never anything smaller than `DEFAULT_RECEIVE_SIZE`.

I guess this has a weird effect if the underlying transport likes to return more than `DEFAULT_RECEIVE_SIZE`. Say it gives us 65 KiB, while `DEFAULT_RECEIVE_SIZE` is 64 KiB. On our first call to `SSLStream.receive_some`, the buffer size is zero, so we do `read(64 KiB)`. This drains 65 KiB from the underlying transport, then decrypts and returns the first 64 KiB. The next time we call `SSLStream.receive_some`, we do `read(64 KiB)` again, but there's already 1 KiB of data in the buffer, so we just return that immediately without refilling the buffer. Then this repeats indefinitely, so we alternate between doing a big receive and a small receive every time. Seems wasteful – it'd be better to return 65 KiB each time.

So maybe a better strategy would be to start with some smallish default receive size, and then increase it over time if we observe the underlying transport giving us more data.

The getters in the `IVirtualization` class haven't changed the fact that `LocalHost.py` either has to have knowledge of the virtualisation attributes type or have a certain level of if/elsery to catch all cases. What I was suggesting would see these lines here reduced to the following:

```python
if job.virtualization is not None:
    script = job.virtualization.modify_script(script)
```
Where you can call the actual method what you like.

---
To achieve this I was suggesting the base class will have an abstract method like so:
```python
class IVirtualization:
...
def modify_script(self, script):
        return script.replace('###VIRTUALIZATION###', repr(self.__class__.__name__))\
                     .replace('###VIRTUALIZATIONIMAGE###', repr(self.imageUrl))
...
```

And the derived classes like `Docker` for example would override this hence using polymorphism instead of if/else/else/else ... for however many derived classes we end up making:

```python
Class Docker(IVirtualization):
...

def modify_script(self, script):
    return super(Docker, self).modify_script(script)\
                              .replace('###VIRTUALIZATIONMODE###', repr(self.mode))
...
```

This way there is no implicit knowledge of the type of the virtualization within `LocalHost.py`, no if /elsery  and everything is self contained, modular and scalable.

We seem to need it to be a separate header file (to avoid include loops). I would also like it  to be in `indirect_sender.hpp` itself, but could not find a good to make it work.

- We want `IndirectSender` to be the one to define the context content, but `DataPollHandelr` be the class to provide the object/buffer for it.
- `indirect_sender.hpp` needs to include `data_poll_handler.hpp` (since it defines a member variable of type `DataPollHander` and it needs to know the full type definition).
- On other hand, the `data_poll_hanlder.hpp` needs to know the full `FrameContext` definition to be able to define member variable of this type within `DataPollHandler` class itself. 
- We need full types in both cases, so forward declerations won't work.
- So having the context be defined in a separate header file enable us to include it in `data_poll_hanlder.hpp` and avoid include header file loop.

I think this might be more easily and explicitly done by creating a new `SignalHandler` subclass in `python_repl.py` with the lock and flag, then using `with ExceptionSink.trapped_signals(sigint_toggling_signal_handler):` there, instead of reaching in and mutating the global signal handler field, or moving signal handling concerns *into* `ExceptionSink` itself. This seems to be more testable, less coupled, and less spooky, and makes it clear when looking at the python repl task that some exclusive locking which modifies global state is going on.

This solution seems to work great as is -- my main goal with the global state in `ExceptionSink` was to make global state changes explicit and avoid mixing application-specific and process-handling logic. `SignalHandler` was an attempt to move task-specific or process-specific logic out of `ExceptionSink` and decouple it from the way we actually exit or log errors. Since it seems like `ExceptionSink.trapped_signals()` overwriting the existing signal handling provides the temporary override we want, it seems reasonable to use that approach instead of creating a new interface which reaches into the signal handler base class to modify it.

@hausdorff These are good questions, that we should try to answer. My high-level feedback is that we should consider building on top of what the language provides, so that we can be as idiomatic as possible in a given language and fit-in well with its ecosystem. (And even potentially aligning with future language proposals, much in the same way that TypeScript does).

> Is there any advantage to using these protocols other than just being able to use `for`?

In addition to `for-of`, I believe it also enables destructuring and the spread operator (...) for iterables. There are proposals that expand on iterators that appear to very much align with our needs:

 - [Asynchronous Iterators for JavaScript](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-async-iteration)
 - [Iterator Helpers](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-iterator-helpers)

Implementing using iterators and/or async iterators would enable all sorts of third party "iterator helpers" to _just work_ with what we provide, without us having to provide an implementation or adapters ourselves.

Underneath, we try and perform uploads in parallel with each thread using the `buffer-size` that was set (code [permalink](https://github.com/dnanexus/dx-toolkit/blob/a86d952d5142dc01a18d64f50d5049a0f7e39dbe/src/python/dxpy/bindings/dxfile.py#L513)). We submit these to task to worker pools. Which AFAIK is unaware of the current memory on the worker. What can happen is:

```
num_active_threads * buffer-size > memory availble on machine
```

While your change is less prone failing to upload in `partLimits` I think you're more prone to making the worker going OOM.

My initial idea for a fix is:
1. Make the initial `buffer-size` calculation _slightly_ worker aware (i.e never choose a buffer size greater than the memory on the worker).
2. Modify writes to be aware of the pool and current memory on the machine.

I learned something here... There are a lot of old strings that use `printf` style formatting, like `('percent complete %f%%' % count/100)`. I used this so much I assumed % always had to be escaped with another %, but that's only the case if you do the % replacement i.e. `'this is fine %'` `'but this crashes % %f' % 0.1`.

Looking into `rgb2ycbcr`, it calls `_prepare_color_array` internally which uses `img_as_float` and thus does not automatically rescale floating point inputs. The docstring states that: `Y is between 16 and 235.`, but this is not true for floating point inputs unless they are in [0, 1].

example:
```Python
# uint8 case
np.max(skimage.color.rgb2ycbcr(data.chelsea()))
Out[75]: 182.74402352941178

# floats in range [0, 255.]
np.max(skimage.color.rgb2ycbcr(data.chelsea().astype(float)))
Out[76]: 42535.726
```

For that reason, it is good that we are also rescaling the YCbCr channels again in the inner channel denoising loop. The question is how to best handle sigma in this case:

Perhaps we should apply a global sigma scaling across all channels as opposed to doing per-channel sigma scaling as is being done currently in this PR.  That would be more consistent with the current YCbCr description in the denoise_wavelet docstring.

So, if we didn't use the `Pipe` abstraction, that's what we would have to do. However, pipes are not closed when the write end is closed. Instead, the `select` in `NailgunStreamWriter` will just not return the read fd ever. This makes the cases "I haven't read anything yet" and "I have finished reading and the pipe is closed" indistinguishable for `NSW` as it stands before this PR. 

Therefore, the `PipedNailgunStreamWriter` would have to keep track of the write file descriptors to be able to tell when a pipe is closed. That's feasible, but given that we have the `Pipe` abstraction, I thought it best to use that for readability.

> Aha - in that case, the added test should probably run ./pants test rather than ./pants run?

The invocation of `./pants run` in the integration test is testing a separate issue, which is also fixed in this PR. The use of `SelectInterpreterClientMixin` here is intended so that the interpreter pinning from `PytestRun` in #7563 could be used in `PythonRun`, which fixes this separate interpreter selection failure in `PythonRun`.

The reason this change couples those two fixes is that the interpreter selection failure in `PythonRun` that this change fixes doesn't occur with any random package I could find on pypi when testing -- specifically `google-cloud-bigquery` (all versions I tried) happened to also be the repro case for both the `prepend_to_pythonpath=True` fix for `PytestRun`, as well as this separate interpreter selection fix for `PythonRun`.

I don't at all understand why this `google-cloud-bigquery` package (several other related google cloud packages as well, unfortunately) would be causing the interpreter selection failure specifically, I really cannot see what they are doing wrong to cause that issue and it may also repro for other packages I haven't tried. But it is ~clear (from the pex warning message which says exactly this) that they should be putting `setuptools` in their `install_requires`, and they're not. I don't know how bad that is or whether it would be relevant to the `PythonRun` interpreter selection issue that this change also fixes. I looked for some time for a way to somehow invoke pex or create a pex differently to make sure different versions of setuptools were available, and this was the only workaround I could find.

One of the remaining unknowns to me is where this broke, since our monorepo has this dependency running just fine in pants 1.15.x. I was not able to identify any behavior which differed from pex `1.6.5` to `1.6.6` (although I am less familiar with pex than I could be), so something changed in pants. I would love to fix that, but haven't figured it out yet.

Perhaps a TODO today is to try to bisect the change in behavior before trying to merge this.
> Presumably the unit test passed before this change, as well as after?

The statement `from google.cloud import bigquery` would fail if run in a python test before this change with `pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound` -- so this integration test would have failed, but failed at import time, before running tests. That's what `prepend_to_pythonpath=True` works around. The integration test then does `./pants run` in order to test the separate `PythonRun` issue this change also fixes.
> This change should apply anytime this library is used in a PEX.

Well, the `prepend_to_pythonpath` fix actually doesn't appear to be necessary outside of `PytestRun`, which is strange since we're using `self.create_pex()` in `PytestPrep` in a very normal way. It's not yet clear to me why this is the case.

Oh, I overlooked the part about comparing same type sequences (because that doesn't make sense - if both are sequences, then why insist on the same type? Non-generator sequences are already ordered by default, e.g. lists and tuples).

But the code is still incorrect for the case when sequences are actually compared (for loop).

Consider in Python2:
```
>>> range(2,4) < range(0,3)
False
```
and in Python3:
```
>>> SortKey(range(2,4)) < SortKey(range(0,3))                                                      
True
```

Surely it would make sense for `[2,3]` not to be less than `[0,1,2]`. And actually:
```
>>> SortKey(list(range(2,4))) < SortKey(list(range(0,3)))                                          
False
```

Your **code fails because in case the first sequence (element by element) is not less then the second, you failback to comparing lengths** (which you should only if one sequence is exact prefix of the other).

The problem that causes us to pass `squeeze_self` and `squeeze_other`/`broadcast` to the backend, is that on the query compiler level we can't determine whether this is a Series or a single-column frame. On a backend, we have common binary functions that deals with DataFrames and Series at the same time, but its behavior depends on an object type to which we apply a binary function (DataFrame or Series)
<details><summary>Example</summary>


```python
>>> df1
   col1
0     0
1     1
2     2
3     3
>>> arr
array([10, 20, 30, 40])
>>> df1 + arr  # can't do '__add__' with DataFrame and 1D numpy array
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Shared\Python37_64\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\ops\__init__.py", line 651, in f
    self, other = _align_method_FRAME(self, other, axis, flex=True, level=level)
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Shared\Python37_64\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\ops\__init__.py", line 474, in _align_method_FRAME
    right = to_series(right)
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Shared\Python37_64\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\ops\__init__.py", line 466, in to_series
    msg.format(req_len=len(left.columns), given_len=len(right))
ValueError: Unable to coerce to Series, length must be 1: given 4
>>> df1.squeeze() + arr
0    10
1    21
2    32
3    43
Name: col1, dtype: int64
```


</details>

So the workaround for this problem is passing parameters to a backend to indicate what we're dealing with (Frame/Series).

    def compute_expected_predictions_mean(tree_df: pd.DataFrame):

        leaf_values = tree_df['value']
        leaf_weights = tree_df['weight']

I think it is worth calling out that wheels (and the final installed package) contain only the data files that are contained *within* listed `package` directories, *and* only when `include_package_data` is True.  Specifically this can be used to include a top-level `tests` folder in an `sdist` using `MANIFEST.in`.  But by not including `tests` in the `packages` metadata (by using explicit packages or `include`/`exclude` with `find_packages`, you avoid shipping the tests in the wheel and polluting `site-packages` with a `tests` folder.  The tests in the sdist can be used by downstream packagers like Linux distros or conda-forge to test the package before re-packaging.  Maybe this should go in the PyPUG itself?

This is an excellent idea and solution. It has maximum flexibility but has a clear interface and simple implementation, and is thus most reliable.

>     * (which will be copied unless `path/to/files` **is** the workspace dir)

Not quite: IIUC, files will be copied unless they exist exactly _under (expanded) `url`_ in the workspace directory. So in your example, because `regex` differs from `url`, they will be copied even if `path/to/files` is the workdir.

> On the other hand, bulk renaming files with unix tools and fine-tuning the regex is probably easier.

I agree: more elaborate schemes can easily be constructed with a simple (and fast) shell loop. `bulk-add` only needs to be the last step. 

But there's a catch: I believe one still needs an additional option to allow **skipping** files that don't match the regex. This could be for another run of `bulk-add`, or just for files that we don't care about (presentation, logs, meta-data etc).

For example, to import the old (first-generation zip-file) OCR-D GT directories, one could then do:
```
ocrd workspace bulk-add --skip --regex '^.*/(?P<dispname>[^/]*)/(?P=dispname)_(?P<pageid>[0-9]*)\.tif$' --file-id 'FILE_ORIG_{{ pageid }}' --page-id 'PHYS_{{ pageid }}' --file-grp OCR-D-IMG --url '{{ dispname }}_{{ pageid }}.tif' # only images, no copying
ocrd workspace bulk-add --skip --regex '^.*/(?P<dispname>[^/]*)/page/(?P=dispname)_(?P<pageid>[0-9]*)\.xml$' --file-id 'FILE_GT_{{ pageid }}' --page-id 'PHYS_{{ pageid }}' --file-grp OCR-D-GT-SEG-PAGE --url 'page/{{ dispname }}_{{ pageid }}.xml' # only PAGE, no copying
ocrd workspace bulk-add --skip --regex '^.*/(?P<dispname>[^/]*)/alto/(?P=dispname)_(?P<pageid>[0-9]*)\.xml$' --file-id 'FILE_GT-ALTO_{{ pageid }}' --page-id 'PHYS_{{ pageid }}' --file-grp OCR-D-GT-ALTO-SEG-PAGE --mimetype application/alto+xml --url 'alto/{{ dispname }}_{{ pageid }}.xml' # only ALTO, no copying
```
(You cannot match the non-existing image subdirectory as fileGrp in this convention directly, and breaking it up allows a basic form of string transformation.)

If someone does `patronictl edit-config` and changes for example the `database` for an existing logical slot the following happens:
- primary will notice that the existing slot is created in the "wrong" database and try to remove it. If the slot is active `pg_drop_replication_slot()` will fail.
- replica will notice that the existing slot is created in the "wrong" database and removes it. After that it connects to the primary, gets the replication slot file, stops postgres, writes the slot file to PGDATA, and starts postgres. But, since the slot on the primary exists in the "wrong" database, slot on the replica is also wrong, and the action repeates.

So the old way of:

```
--nodeTypes=node-type1,node-type2
--workers=2,2

would still spawn strictly two static nodes for each, but:

```
--nodeTypes=node-type1,node-type2
--workers=0-2,0-2

would imply and spawn autoscaling nodes with a min of 0 and a max of 2.  I think that the defined behavior is then implicit in the count definition and is more intuitive to the user.  I also think that reducing the number of options might make the interface cleaner.

> i'm a little unclear why these complete_count values for each of the tasks differ in quantity. Could you clarify that for me?

Sure!

The complete count with `cache_task_completion=True` of (5, 4, 3) for (`a10`, `a11`, `a12`) is due to luigi's assumption of idempotence of `run()` methods that yield dynamic dependencies in that the worker invokes `run()` and in case it's a generator, it get's the next result and

- if it's a bunch of already complete tasks (some of the completeness checks is happening here), it gets the next generator result, or
- if it's a bunch of tasks of which at least one is not complete yet, it adds all of them to the tree and forgets about the state of the generator.

([code here](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/a295041d9c344135ef7f8d41e26f6fdbe6e55c58/luigi/worker.py#L139-L154)) The yielding task is placed back to the tree in PENDING state, too. And when it's started again later on, the entire procedure is triggered again, leading to a new generator, but now with the previously incomplete bunch being complete. Therefore, completion checks of tasks of a certain bunch are always performed at least once more than those in the next bunch.

> Similarly, i'm confused as to why the assertions below (with check_complete_on_run=True) resulted in larger complete_count quantities.

With `check_complete_on_run=True` there is a single, additional call happening [here](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/a295041d9c344135ef7f8d41e26f6fdbe6e55c58/luigi/worker.py#L195).

> I guess my point is that, if I ask someone to write a unit test for exactly what you said "This test checks that not providing any arguments will cause the help message to be displayed.", no one would ever monkeypatch the platform.

I respectfully disagree. A unit test by definition is a test to validate functionality *in isolation*. If a unit test's result depends on what platform runs, it is not a unit test. Unit tests are supposed to test that the logic in the *unit under test* is correct and disregard all external factors (e.g. no external I/O or calls to anything which might do I/O should be allowed). The fact that often times such tests need to know implementation details in order to patch out external calls is inevitable and it becomes largely subjective to decide how many implementation details are we OK to accept in these tests. If the code is structured well, little mocking should be necessary, but this is not always the case and minimal changes to unit tests due to implementation changes are perfectly fine in my opinion.

This process seems like it's trying to do too much to combine PoW and PoB into comparable units. Why not just compare ranks?

Thinking about how this is implemented with sortition, it would look something like:

```
W_ib = scaled burn amount for candidate i = (burn_i / burn_total)
W_iw = scaled work amount for candidate i = (work_i / work_total)

t := "proof-of-work" tuning factor
T_i = tuned score for candidate i = (1-t)(W_ib) + t(W_iw)
```

And then using `T_i` to claim ranges over the sortition space. This way, the tunable proofs don't need to try and compare the amount of work going into one component versus the other -- it just lets the competitive aspect of mining balance that.

Thank you for asking @brian-smith-tcril . I mentioned the following in the commit:
```
Tests for `extract` script were skipped in the old days because they were part of `edx-platform` tests.
So now since we have this code in a separate package; it is more convenient to reactivate
these tests and eliminate the run-once restriction
```
for the same reason, tests were built to take less time (before they were skipped later). The approach of using a global variable to control the flow is terrible in modern code for many reasons:

* For class setup we should use `setUpClass` that will run only once instead of using a global variable like this. This is a very old code before `setUpClass` was introduced in Python 2.7
* The setup uses `_setup_i18n_test_config` which will [revert files](https://github.com/openedx/i18n-tools/blob/121700404d20a692326c8c1df0a2707f38117b61/tests/__init__.py#L24) after **each test**, not after all tests. So the first test to run will include changes of the `extract` command, while all other tests will not. Tests were passing because the [source files](https://github.com/openedx/i18n-tools/tree/723a0fbc3158afe9d0c42f4d5ed2cc6315ce480c/tests/data/mock-django-app/locale/mock/LC_MESSAGES) before doing `extract` are already formatted correctly. Meaning: they were not testing the correct files anyway except for the first test to run
* Because of the second point: my new test will fail if another test runs before it. Simply because files will be reverted and the metadata will be found in the file

So, we can edit the source files and keep the run-once mechanism, but that will not fix the fact that tests are not testing the correct files (as described in the second point above)

Backfill status is an overloaded term.

The original intent of this column is purely to track state on the behalf of the backfill daemon.  The backfill daemon has completed its work for a given backfill job if it has requested a run for every partition.  It can then filter by this column to find more work that it should do.

This is completely separate from the user-facing concept of backfill status (i.e. have all the runs completed successfully for a backfill).  We make sure the UI reflects the individual run status rather than just reading from the backfill status column.

For asset backfills, even though the particulars are different, we should still maintain this split between the concept of "complete" from the daemon perspective (all runs requested), and from the user perspective (backfill is done).

I'm not as up to speed on the asset backfill particulars (the logic is quite complicated and involves the cache queryer).  If a run fails, will the backfill retry?  If not, then I think "complete" for the daemon means that all runs are requested.  That ensures that we won't request duplicate runs on the next backfill iteration.

This happens _all the time_, and the `EnvironmentVarsRequest` API is brittle -- you have to request the specific env vars ahead of time, and then pass them into the method that consumes them. This makes those subsystems very difficult to consume correctly. Pre-fetching the envvars makes that entirely non-brittle.

That said, the intention here is that `EnvironmentAware.env_vars` is meant to be used solely for those post-processing methods. Would it help if `env_vars` were called `_env_vars` instead?

We really only want to instrument `pulumi` CLI here on the server side, and only instrument the clients in the Pulumi CLI. An ENV var would propagate to all sub-processes and enable tracing in servers created via generic Serve. We don't want those traces, if they do come we need FS-level locking when persisting them to a file, or else to disambiguate the file name by process ID. 

It's not possible to solve this more cleanly and directly without taking some deeper changes around our API, with some complicated dance to avoid breaking changes. This is because the generic Serve* function is used to construct all kinds of servers, including  servers for resource providers. What we'd want to do is change the signature of Serve* so that the caller would configure interceptors. So like `ServeWithOptions*` perhaps? And then switch over the call sites to control this.

- We need to track an `startIndex` (how much of the `mOutputString` was previously used and emitted to logs). 
- Such a model would also assume that the CLI outputs will always end at some point with `\n\r` with nothing after it (which may be the case in way we generate output today in the code but I think may be better if we don't rely on it).
- The `memmove()` model allow us to use the entire `mOutputString` buffer for every CLI output line. 
-  Also practically very frequently, `memove()` copies just one byte the `\0` (since most often the `\n\r` is added on its own or at end of single string from an `Output()` method call.

In case it helps, I went through a couple of iterations of the counting code and this was by far the simplest one I came up with - counting connections as "active" once http-parser tells us a message has begun, and then not counting them once we're done with the sending the response.

I contemplated doing something like... storing all of the requests in an array/linked list/hash map on the ThreadInfo struct, and then basically watching for that to drop to either drop to zero entries, or if the existing entries aren't handling any connections. That would eliminate counting bugs, but it would also be significantly more complex as we'd need to handle growing the array occasionally if there's an influx of requests. I think we'd also need to store some additional state on the Request struct for whether or not it's currently handling a request. Given the complexity, that's why I decided to go for the simpler approach :)

That said though, I think it's worth doing some testing on dropping connections halfway through requests at various points in the lifecycle to see how the counting holds up. I'll go back and do that, and report on my findings :)

The `*_handler` arguments are wrong for a Source like fsspec, I agree. (And didn't see it coming.)

If we go the route of handling all remote file I/O with fsspec, then the natural thing to do would be to replace these arguments with a `filesystem` or `fs` argument (as is becoming standard in some other projects).

The role currently played by `file_handler` and `object_handler` would need some equivalent, though. There are two ways one might want to use a `file_handler`: as memmap and as conventional file object(s). I expect that memmap would usually be better, but worse through NFS. For `object_handler`, there's only one option (not something for a user to select), but it is very distinct from handlers for an object that you know is a file: if it's a generic object, we can't assume that we can `seek` and `read` in parallel.

IIRC, the reason why we felt that `deployment` should be an interactive process was that some deploys require interactive password input or keychain access, etc. Not actually invoking these as interactive would break that. It would also potentially break caching I think, because `InteractiveProcess` isn't cached, while `Process` is, so this line will cache deployment processes.

BUT, in many cases a process does _not_ need to be interactive, and that allows things to run in parallel here, which is great.

I think that the best answer is likely something like #8923, as that would disconnect whether a process needs to run in the foreground from whether it can be run in parallel. I also don't think that that one would be that hard to do?

In the meantime though, could a python union type of `process: Process | InteractiveProcess` and then some conditional handling for "has any interactive" work...? Would likely be a pain.

However, `deploy_group` still has some heavy-weight logic left outside the `Client` class. What are your thoughts on making another `deploy_group` function inside the `Client` class and using the global `deploy_group` function as a thin wrapper that parses the `Deployments` and calls the `Client`'s `deploy_group`? This would be similar to how the global `deploy` function and the `Client`'s `deploy` function work together.

Note that one possible downside is that the `Client` class can't directly interface with `Deployment` objects. That means the `Client`'s `deploy_group` would need to take in a list of dictionaries containing the properties for every `Deployment` instead of a list of `Deployment`s. The idea behind DRY-ing this up is that the cqlmapper and cassandra `ConfigParser`-s do the same things, the end object is just different.  If we had to set things on the session object manually, I think it might make sense to share the bulk of the `ConfigParser` code and just plug in the final context factory object so we don't have to keep duplicating the code between the two.

i.e.
```python
class CassandraClient(config.Parser):
    """Configure a CQLMapper client.
     This is meant to be used with
    :py:meth:`baseplate.core.Baseplate.configure_context`.
     See :py:func:`cluster_from_config` for available configurables.
     :param keyspace: Which keyspace to set as the default for operations.
     """

     def __init__(self, keyspace: str, factory_cls=CQLMapperContextFactory, **kwargs):
        self.keyspace = keyspace
        self.kwargs = kwargs
        self.factory_cls = factory_cls

     def parse(self, key_path: str, raw_config: config.RawConfig) -> ContextFactory:
        cluster = cluster_from_config(raw_config, prefix=f"{key_path}.", **self.kwargs)
        session = cluster.connect(keyspace=self.keyspace)
        return self.factory_cls(session)
```
